title: Muzzle velocity

infobox.value: Less than 396 m/s (1,299 ft/s) | Between 910 and 1,070 m/s (3,000–3,500 ft/s) | Greater than 1,070 m/s (3,500 ft/s)
- | Between 470 and 1,020 m/s (1,550–3,350 ft/s) | Greater than 1,020 m/s (3,350 ft/s)
- | Between 1,070 and 1,520 m/s (3,500–5,000 ft/s) | Greater than 1,520 m/s (5,000 ft/s)

is the speed of a [projectile](/wiki/Projectile "Projectile") ([bullet](/wiki/Bullet "Bullet"), [pellet](/wiki/Pellet_(air_gun) "Pellet (air gun)"), [slug](/wiki/Slug_(projectile) "Slug (projectile)"), [ball](/wiki/Round_shot "Round shot")/[shots](/wiki/Shot_(pellet) "Shot (pellet)") or [shell](/wiki/Shell_(projectile) "Shell (projectile)"))[[1]](#cite_note-1) at the moment it leaves the end of a [gun](/wiki/Gun "Gun")'s [barrel](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel") (i.e. the [muzzle](/wiki/Gun_barrel#Muzzle "Gun barrel")).[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Firearm](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm") muzzle velocities range from approximately 120 m/s (390 ft/s) to 370 m/s (1,200 ft/s) in [black powder muskets](/wiki/Musket "Musket"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) to more than 1,200 m/s (3,900 ft/s)[[4]](#cite_note-4) in modern rifles with high-velocity cartridges such as the [.220 Swift](/wiki/.220_Swift ".220 Swift") and [.204 Ruger](/wiki/.204_Ruger ".204 Ruger"), all the way to 1,700 m/s (5,600 ft/s)[[5]](#cite_note-5) for [tank](/wiki/Tank "Tank") guns firing [kinetic energy penetrator](/wiki/Kinetic_energy_penetrator "Kinetic energy penetrator") ammunition. To simulate orbital debris impacts on spacecraft, [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") launches projectiles through [light-gas guns](/wiki/Light-gas_gun "Light-gas gun") at speeds up to 8,500 m/s (28,000 ft/s).[*[relevant?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Stay_on_topic "Wikipedia:Writing better articles")*][[6]](#cite_note-6)
["muzzle velocity"](https://www.thefreedictionary.com/muzzle+velocity). *Dictionary of Military and Associated Terms*. 2005. Retrieved 29 July 2024.

["Muzzle Velocity"](https://web.archive.org/web/20100515022156/http://www.militarydictionary.com/definition/muzzle-velocity.html). Archived from [the original](http://www.militarydictionary.com/definition/muzzle-velocity.html) on 15 May 2010. Retrieved 9 June 2011.
["The Accuracy of Black Powder Muskets"](http://www.willegal.net/iron_brigade/musket.pdf) (PDF). Retrieved 9 June 2011.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Velocity of projectile the moment it leaves the gun barrel

For the video game, see [*Muzzle Velocity* (video game)](/wiki/Muzzle_Velocity_(video_game) "Muzzle Velocity (video game)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Girl_Gunners-_the_work_of_the_Auxiliary_Territorial_Service_at_An_Experimental_Station%2C_Shoeburyness%2C_Essex%2C_England%2C_1943_D12697.jpg/250px-Girl_Gunners-_the_work_of_the_Auxiliary_Territorial_Service_at_An_Experimental_Station%2C_Shoeburyness%2C_Essex%2C_England%2C_1943_D12697.jpg)](/wiki/File:Girl_Gunners-_the_work_of_the_Auxiliary_Territorial_Service_at_An_Experimental_Station,_Shoeburyness,_Essex,_England,_1943_D12697.jpg)

Women of the [Auxiliary Territorial Service](/wiki/Auxiliary_Territorial_Service "Auxiliary Territorial Service") firing a 25 pounder shell at the Royal Artillery experimental station at [Shoeburyness](/wiki/MOD_Shoeburyness "MOD Shoeburyness"). The shells are fired through a velocity screen, which has a grid of copper wire. When the shell is fired through the wire, the circuit is broken, by which the speed of the shell can be checked.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Girl_Gunners-_the_work_of_the_Auxiliary_Territorial_Service_at_An_Experimental_Station%2C_Shoeburyness%2C_Essex%2C_England%2C_1943_D12694.jpg/250px-Girl_Gunners-_the_work_of_the_Auxiliary_Territorial_Service_at_An_Experimental_Station%2C_Shoeburyness%2C_Essex%2C_England%2C_1943_D12694.jpg)](/wiki/File:Girl_Gunners-_the_work_of_the_Auxiliary_Territorial_Service_at_An_Experimental_Station,_Shoeburyness,_Essex,_England,_1943_D12694.jpg)

The velocity screen being disassembled after use.

**Muzzle velocity** is the speed of a [projectile](/wiki/Projectile "Projectile") ([bullet](/wiki/Bullet "Bullet"), [pellet](/wiki/Pellet_(air_gun) "Pellet (air gun)"), [slug](/wiki/Slug_(projectile) "Slug (projectile)"), [ball](/wiki/Round_shot "Round shot")/[shots](/wiki/Shot_(pellet) "Shot (pellet)") or [shell](/wiki/Shell_(projectile) "Shell (projectile)"))[[1]](#cite_note-1) at the moment it leaves the end of a [gun](/wiki/Gun "Gun")'s [barrel](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel") (i.e. the [muzzle](/wiki/Gun_barrel#Muzzle "Gun barrel")).[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Firearm](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm") muzzle velocities range from approximately 120 m/s (390 ft/s) to 370 m/s (1,200 ft/s) in [black powder muskets](/wiki/Musket "Musket"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) to more than 1,200 m/s (3,900 ft/s)[[4]](#cite_note-4) in modern rifles with high-velocity cartridges such as the [.220 Swift](/wiki/.220_Swift ".220 Swift") and [.204 Ruger](/wiki/.204_Ruger ".204 Ruger"), all the way to 1,700 m/s (5,600 ft/s)[[5]](#cite_note-5) for [tank](/wiki/Tank "Tank") guns firing [kinetic energy penetrator](/wiki/Kinetic_energy_penetrator "Kinetic energy penetrator") ammunition. To simulate orbital debris impacts on spacecraft, [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") launches projectiles through [light-gas guns](/wiki/Light-gas_gun "Light-gas gun") at speeds up to 8,500 m/s (28,000 ft/s).[*[relevant?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Stay_on_topic "Wikipedia:Writing better articles")*][[6]](#cite_note-6)
Several factors, including the type of firearm, the cartridge, and the barrel length, determine the bullet's muzzle velocity.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Projectile velocity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muzzle_velocity&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Projectile velocity")]

For projectiles in [unpowered flight](/wiki/Unpowered_flight "Unpowered flight"), its velocity is highest at leaving the muzzle and drops off steadily because of [air resistance](/wiki/Air_resistance "Air resistance"). Projectiles traveling less than the [speed of sound](/wiki/Speed_of_sound "Speed of sound") (about 340 m/s (1,100 ft/s) in dry air at [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level")) are *subsonic*, while those traveling faster are *supersonic* and thus can travel a substantial distance and even hit a target before a nearby observer hears the sound of the weapon being fired. Projectile speed through air depends on a number of factors such as [barometric pressure](/wiki/Barometric_pressure "Barometric pressure"), [humidity](/wiki/Humidity "Humidity"), [air temperature](/wiki/Air_temperature "Air temperature") and [wind speed](/wiki/Wind_speed "Wind speed").

A 1-gram (15-[grain](/wiki/Grain_(unit) "Grain (unit)")) projectile was accelerated to velocities exceeding 9,000 m/s (30,000 ft/s) at [Sandia National Laboratories](/wiki/Sandia_National_Laboratories "Sandia National Laboratories") in 1994. The gun operated in two stages. First, burning gunpowder was used to drive a piston to pressurize hydrogen to 10,000 [atm](/wiki/Atmosphere_(unit) "Atmosphere (unit)") (1.0 GPa). The pressurized gas was then released to a secondary piston, which traveled forward into a shock-absorbing "pillow", transferring the energy from the piston to the projectile on the other side of the pillow.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Conventional guns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muzzle_velocity&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Conventional guns")]

In conventional guns, muzzle velocity is determined by the quantity of the [propellant](/wiki/Propellant "Propellant"), its quality (in terms of chemical [burn speed](/wiki/Burn_rate_(chemistry) "Burn rate (chemistry)") and expansion), the mass of the projectile, and the length of the barrel. A slower-burning propellant needs a longer barrel to finish its burn before leaving, but conversely can use a heavier projectile. This is a mathematical tradeoff.[[9]](#cite_note-rb-9) A faster-burning propellant may accelerate a lighter projectile to higher speeds if the same amount of propellant is used. Within a gun, the [gaseous pressure](/wiki/Pressure "Pressure") created as a result of the combustion process is a limiting factor on projectile velocity. Consequently, propellant quality and quantity, projectile mass, and barrel length must all be balanced to achieve safety and to [optimize](/wiki/Design_Optimization "Design Optimization") performance.

Longer barrels give the propellant force more time to work on propelling the bullet.[[9]](#cite_note-rb-9) For this reason longer barrels generally provide higher velocities, everything else being equal. As the bullet moves down the bore, however, the propellant's gas pressure behind it diminishes. Given a long enough barrel, there would eventually be a point at which friction between the bullet and the barrel, and air resistance, would equal the force of the gas pressure behind it, and from that point, the velocity of the bullet would decrease.

## Rifles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muzzle_velocity&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Rifles")]

Rifled barrels have spiral twists carved inside them that spin the bullet so that it remains stable in flight. This mechanism is known as [rifling](/wiki/Rifling "Rifling"). Longer barrels provide more opportunity to rotate the bullet before it leaves the gun. Provided there's enough rifling in the barrel to adequately stabilize a particular round, there is no appreciable increase in precision with increasing barrel length. Longer barrels make it easier to aim if using iron sights, because of the longer sight radius, and with the right propellant load they can increase muzzle velocity, which gives a flatter trajectory and reduces the need to adjust for range.

A bullet, while moving through its barrel, is being pushed forward by the gas expanding behind it. This gas is created following the [trigger](/wiki/Trigger_(firearms) "Trigger (firearms)") being pulled, causing the [firing pin](/wiki/Firing_pin "Firing pin") to strike the [primer](/wiki/Primer_(firearms) "Primer (firearms)"), which in turn ignites the solid propellant packed inside the [bullet cartridge](/wiki/Cartridge_(firearms) "Cartridge (firearms)"), making it combust while situated in the [chamber](/wiki/Chamber_(firearms) "Chamber (firearms)"). Once it leaves the barrel, the force of the expanding gas ceases to propel the bullet forth.[[10]](#cite_note-10) When a bullet is fired from a handgun with a 2-inch (51 mm) barrel, the bullet only has a 2-inch (51 mm) "runway" to be spun before it leaves the barrel. Likewise, it has only a 2-inch (51 mm) space in which to accelerate before it must fly without any additional force behind it. In some instances, the powder may not have even been fully burned in guns with short barrels. So, the muzzle velocity of a 2-inch (51 mm) barrel is less than that of a 4-inch (100 mm) barrel, which is less than that of a 6-inch (150 mm) barrel.

Large [naval guns](/wiki/Naval_artillery "Naval artillery") will have high length-to-diameter ratios, ranging between 38:1 to 50:1. This length ratio maximizes the projectile velocity. There is much interest in modernizing naval weaponry by using electrically powered [railguns](/wiki/Railgun "Railgun"), which shoot projectiles using an electromagnetic pulse. These overcome the limitations noted above. With these railguns, a constant acceleration is provided along the entire length of the device by means of the electromagnetic pulse. This greatly increases the muzzle velocity. Another significant advantage of railguns is not requiring explosive propellant.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The result of this is that a ship will not need to transport propellant and that a land-station will not have to maintain an inventory of it either. Explosive propellant, stored in large quantities, is susceptible to explosion.[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12) While this can be mitigated with safety precautions,[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12) railguns eschew the need for such measures altogether. Even the projectile's internal charges may be eliminated due to the already high velocity. This means the projectile becomes a strictly [kinetic](/wiki/Kinetic_energy "Kinetic energy") weapon.

## Categories of velocity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muzzle_velocity&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Categories of velocity")]

The [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") defines different categories of muzzle velocity for different classes of weapons:[[13]](#cite_note-DA-13)

| Weapon | Low velocity | High velocity | [Hypervelocity](/wiki/Hypervelocity "Hypervelocity") |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") [cannons](/wiki/Cannon "Cannon") | Less than 396 m/s (1,299 ft/s) | Between 910 and 1,070 m/s (3,000–3,500 ft/s) | Greater than 1,070 m/s (3,500 ft/s) |
| [Tank guns](/wiki/Tank_gun "Tank gun") | - | Between 470 and 1,020 m/s (1,550–3,350 ft/s) | Greater than 1,020 m/s (3,350 ft/s) |
| [Small arms](/wiki/Small_arms "Small arms") | - | Between 1,070 and 1,520 m/s (3,500–5,000 ft/s) | Greater than 1,520 m/s (5,000 ft/s) |
